refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows design parameters (e.g., hydraulic response time, travel speed authority, load sensing) to be evaluated independently of operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution does not introduce artificial delays or constraints that prevent the cycle from completing within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time (load initiation to dump completion) across a statistically significant sample (n ≥ 30 cycles) and confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. Separately validate that operator and tele-operator control interfaces support the timing demands without introducing latency bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize transition delays between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, each optimized for its operational constraints.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle controller that orchestrates all sub-operations via state machine logic, with feedback loops to detect and compensate for delays in real-time.

DP3: Design the powertrain and actuation system to deliver peak performance (acceleration, speed, force) sufficient to complete each sub-operation within its allocated time budget under worst-case site conditions.
DP4: Establish a predictive load-balancing strategy that pre-positions the vehicle and adjusts operational sequencing based on site topology and material properties to minimize idle time.
DP5: Implement redundant or parallel execution paths for critical sub-operations (e.g., dual hydraulic circuits for dump) to reduce single-point delays and improve p95 resilience.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP2 + DP3 (integrated): Decompose the cycle into independent timing budgets (DP1), orchestrate via state machine control with real-time feedback (DP2), and size powertrain/actuation to meet peak demands (DP3). This combination is solution-neutral, supports modular verification, and avoids over-specification of redundancy or predictive logic at this stage.

potential_sources: DP1 ↔ DP3: Timing budgets (DP1) are interdependent with powertrain capability (DP3); if powertrain is undersized, budgets become infeasible. Mitigation: Establish powertrain sizing as a constraint input to DP1 decomposition.
DP2 ↔ DP1: State machine logic (DP2) must respect the sequential and parallel structure implied by DP1; tight coupling if state transitions are not cleanly mapped to sub-operation boundaries.
DP2 ↔ DP3: Real-time feedback loops (DP2) require sensor and actuation interfaces from DP3; loose coupling if interfaces are well-defined (e.g., standardized sensor outputs, command protocols).
Site conditions (constraint): Worst-case site topology and material properties affect feasibility of all DPs; not fully decoupled from design logic.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP set exhibits acceptable coupling because each DP addresses a distinct concern (temporal decomposition, control logic, physical capability). However, the mapping is sensitive to the accuracy of timing budgets and powertrain assumptions; iteration is expected during detailed design.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Timing budgets for load, travel, and dump can be independently estimated and summed without significant interaction losses (e.g., acceleration/deceleration overlap, material settling time). Risk: If sub-operations are tightly coupled (e.g., dump must begin before travel completes), budget allocation becomes non-linear and DP1 decomposition fails. Mitigation: Validate cycle structure with customer and site trials early.

Assumption: Powertrain and actuation system can be sized to meet peak demands (e.g., maximum load mass, steepest grade, highest ambient temperature) without exceeding cost or packaging constraints. Risk: If sizing is infeasible, cycle time target becomes unachievable. Mitigation: Establish powertrain feasibility study as a gate before finalizing DP1 budgets.

Assumption: Real-time feedback and state machine control can detect and compensate for delays within the cycle time window (e.g., sensor latency, actuation response time are negligible relative to sub-operation duration). Risk: If latency is significant, feedback loops may introduce instability or miss compensation opportunities. Mitigation: Characterize sensor and actuation response times; validate control loop stability in simulation.

Assumption: Site conditions (topology, material properties, operator skill) remain within the agreed duty cycle envelope. Risk: If actual conditions exceed assumptions, cycle time target may not be met. Mitigation: Define duty cycle limits explicitly; implement monitoring to detect out-of-envelope operation.

Assumption: p95 acceptance criterion is achievable without redundancy or parallel execution paths. Risk: If variability in site conditions or component performance is high, p95 may be difficult to meet with a single execution path. Mitigation: Collect baseline cycle time data; assess variability; consider DP5 (redundancy) if p95 margin is insufficient.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state the acceptable variability in cycle time (e.g., p95 ≤ CT_target implies a distribution; quantify the p5 and median targets as well) and the duty cycle envelope (load mass range, site grade range, material types, ambient conditions). This will enable tighter coupling analysis and reduce risk of DP infeasibility. Additionally, decompose DP2 (state machine control) into sub-DPs for sensor integration, actuation command generation, and feedback compensation to make the control architecture more analyzable. Consider a phased approach: DP1 + DP3 for initial feasibility; DP2 for refinement if baseline cycle time is close to target.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates powertrain/actuation sizing, cycle timing decomposition, and state machine orchestration with real-time feedback for autonomous operation under teleoperation oversight.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission/cycle directives and timing constraints from teleoperation or autonomous planner.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports real-time state, actuation feedback, cycle progress, and timing budget utilization to teleoperation and monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and fault signals; exports state machine status and actuation limits for safety arbitration.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Carries Tasking, Telemetry, and SafetyAlert over communication medium to/from remote operator and safety services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP1 (timing budgets) and DP3 (powertrain/actuation sizing) are intrinsic to vehicle design and control. DP2 (state machine + real-time feedback) is naturally housed in vehicle control logic. Minor diffusion: safety arbitration logic may require coordination with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface, but core orchestration remains in Vehicle.

Poor fit. Safety Services should enforce constraints and monitor, not own timing budgets, powertrain sizing, or primary state machine. DP1, DP2, DP3 are operational/control concerns, not safety-specific. High diffusion: timing and actuation logic would leak into Vehicle anyway.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a remote interface/command authority, not a container for vehicle control logic. DP1, DP2, DP3 must execute on-board the Vehicle for real-time responsiveness and safety. High diffusion: timing budgets and state machine would be split between remote operator and vehicle, violating latency and autonomy requirements.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution likely refers to a higher-level system or platform integrator. DP1, DP2, DP3 are Vehicle-specific operational concerns. Placing them in NGHE Solution would create unnecessary abstraction and diffusion; Vehicle would still need local implementations.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of DP1 + DP2 + DP3 because it directly controls powertrain/actuation (DP3), manages real-time cycle timing (DP1), and executes the state machine orchestration (DP2). All three DPs are tightly coupled to on-board control logic and must respond to real-time feedback with minimal latency. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) allow the Vehicle to receive mission directives and safety constraints while exporting state and feedback, without diffusing core control logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services requires veto authority over state transitions or actuation commands, the boundary between Vehicle state machine and Safety Services arbitration may blur. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface as a constraint input (e.g., max speed, disable actuation) that Vehicle state machine respects, rather than allowing Safety Services to own state transitions.

Risk: If Teleoperation demands real-time override of timing budgets or state machine, latency and consistency may suffer. Mitigation: Establish clear authority model: Vehicle executes autonomous state machine; Teleoperation can issue new Tasking directives (e.g., abort, new cycle) but does not micro-manage state transitions.

Risk: Powertrain/actuation sizing (DP3) may depend on mission profile or environmental constraints not fully known at Vehicle design time. Mitigation: Parameterize sizing within Vehicle module; allow Tasking to specify cycle constraints that Vehicle uses to validate or adapt actuation limits at runtime.

Risk: Real-time feedback loop (DP2) may require sensor fusion or state estimation that could be owned by a separate perception/estimation module. Mitigation: Define clear sensor input interfaces; Vehicle owns state machine logic and feedback control, but receives processed sensor data from external modules via well-defined interfaces.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP1 + DP2 + DP3 is well-contained in the Vehicle module. However, to strengthen the architecture, explicitly define the following sub-interfaces within Vehicle to clarify internal decomposition: (1) Sensor Input Interface: Vehicle receives processed state estimates (position, velocity, actuation status) from perception/estimation modules. (2) Actuation Command Interface: Vehicle outputs control signals to powertrain/actuation hardware. (3) Constraint Input Interface: Vehicle receives safety limits and mission parameters via Tasking and SafetyAlert. This internal clarity will prevent diffusion as the design matures.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP2 + DP3 (timing budgets, state machine orchestration with real-time feedback, and powertrain/actuation sizing) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR requirement to execute the load→travel→dump cycle within customer-defined cycle time targets. The decomposition is solution-neutral, avoids over-specification, and supports modular verification. Each DP is necessary and together they are sufficient to achieve the functional goal.

diffusion_risks: Safety arbitration logic may require coordination with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface; mitigation: define SafetyAlert as constraint input (max speed, disable actuation) that Vehicle state machine respects, not as shared ownership of state transitions.

Teleoperation may demand real-time override of timing budgets or state machine; mitigation: establish clear authority model where Vehicle executes autonomous state machine and Teleoperation issues new Tasking directives (abort, new cycle) without micro-managing transitions.

Powertrain/actuation sizing (DP3) may depend on mission profile or environmental constraints unknown at Vehicle design time; mitigation: parameterize sizing within Vehicle module and allow Tasking to specify cycle constraints for runtime validation.

Real-time feedback loop (DP2) may require sensor fusion or state estimation from external perception modules; mitigation: define clear sensor input interfaces where Vehicle owns state machine logic but receives processed sensor data via well-defined boundaries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion score. DP1 (timing budgets) and DP3 (powertrain/actuation sizing) are intrinsic to vehicle design and control. DP2 (state machine + real-time feedback) is naturally housed in vehicle control logic. The Vehicle module is the clear ownership candidate because it directly controls powertrain/actuation, manages real-time cycle timing, and executes state machine orchestration with minimal latency. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) enable mission directives and safety constraints without diffusing core control logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'Measured cycle time p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly verifiable at the Vehicle module boundary via Telemetry interface export of cycle progress and timing budget utilization. DP1 (timing budgets) provides the decomposition framework; DP2 (state machine + real-time feedback) enables on-board measurement; DP3 (powertrain/actuation sizing) ensures physical capability. Verification can be conducted through instrumented testing of the Vehicle module under representative worksite conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle module owns DP1, DP2, DP3 and is responsible for meeting the FR. Tasking interface receives mission/cycle directives and timing constraints from teleoperation or autonomous planner. Telemetry interface exports real-time state, actuation feedback, cycle progress, and timing budget utilization for monitoring and verification. SafetyAlert interface receives safety constraints and fault signals; Vehicle respects these as control limits. This allocation supports traceability from FR through DP to IM and enables clear responsibility assignment for cycle time achievement.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define three sub-interfaces within the Vehicle module to prevent diffusion as design matures: (1) Sensor Input Interface—Vehicle receives processed state estimates (position, velocity, actuation status) from perception/estimation modules; (2) Actuation Command Interface—Vehicle outputs control signals to powertrain/actuation hardware; (3) Constraint Input Interface—Vehicle receives safety limits and mission parameters via Tasking and SafetyAlert.

Formalize the authority model in the Tasking interface specification: Vehicle executes autonomous state machine; Teleoperation can issue new Tasking directives (abort, new cycle, updated timing constraints) but does not micro-manage state transitions. This prevents latency and consistency issues.

Parameterize powertrain/actuation sizing (DP3) within Vehicle module to accept mission profile and environmental constraints at runtime via Tasking interface, enabling validation and adaptation of actuation limits without redesign.

Define SafetyAlert interface semantics explicitly: Safety Services provides constraint inputs (e.g., max speed, disable actuation, fault signals) that Vehicle state machine respects as control limits, rather than allowing Safety Services to own or veto state transitions. This preserves Vehicle ownership of DP2 while ensuring safety compliance.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the Vehicle module achieves strong containment (0.92). However, the gate analysis identifies four critical diffusion risks and recommends explicit sub-interface definitions and authority model formalization. Given the high safety criticality of this load→travel→dump cycle execution, revising the current level to harden interface contracts and authority boundaries is essential before proceeding to next-level decomposition. Stopping prematurely would leave ambiguity in how Vehicle interacts with Safety Services, Teleoperation, and sensor fusion—risks that will compound during detailed design. Proceeding to sub-FRs without clarifying these boundaries risks diffusion of state machine ownership and safety arbitration logic.

revise.why: DP2 (state machine orchestration with real-time feedback) and DP3 (powertrain/actuation sizing) require explicit sub-interface specifications to prevent diffusion. Specifically: (1) Formalize DP2 to include three sub-interfaces: Sensor Input Interface (Vehicle receives processed state estimates), Actuation Command Interface (Vehicle outputs control signals), and Constraint Input Interface (Vehicle receives safety limits and mission parameters). (2) Clarify authority model: Vehicle executes autonomous state machine; Teleoperation issues new Tasking directives (abort, new cycle, updated timing constraints) but does not micro-manage transitions. (3) Parameterize DP3 to accept mission profile and environmental constraints at runtime via Tasking interface. (4) Define SafetyAlert interface semantics: Safety Services provides constraint inputs (max speed, disable actuation, fault signals) that Vehicle respects as control limits, not as veto authority over state transitions. These revisions harden the DP specification without changing the fundamental decomposition logic, ensuring that when sub-FRs are derived, they operate within clear ownership and interface boundaries.

assumptions: Vehicle module has direct control over powertrain and actuation hardware with deterministic latency ≤ 100 ms for state transitions.
Sensor fusion and state estimation are performed by external perception modules; Vehicle receives processed state estimates via well-defined Sensor Input Interface.
Safety Services operates as a constraint provider (via SafetyAlert interface) rather than a co-owner of state machine logic; Vehicle respects safety limits as control bounds.
Teleoperation authority is limited to issuing new Tasking directives (abort, new cycle, updated timing constraints); Vehicle does not accept micro-management of individual state transitions.
Timing budgets (DP1) are feasible within the powertrain/actuation capabilities parameterized at design time and validated at runtime via Tasking constraints.
Real-time feedback loop (DP2) operates at sufficient frequency (≥10 Hz) to detect and correct deviations from cycle timing targets within acceptable tolerance.
open_risks: Safety arbitration latency: If SafetyAlert constraint updates (e.g., disable actuation) arrive during a state transition, Vehicle must have a clear, deterministic response protocol. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as asynchronous constraint input with immediate effect on actuation limits; state machine respects constraints without re-entering previous states.

Teleoperation override authority: If Teleoperation issues conflicting directives (e.g., abort while Vehicle is mid-dump), Vehicle must have a clear priority and recovery logic. Mitigation: formalize Tasking interface to specify that new directives supersede prior ones; Vehicle transitions to safe state (e.g., hold position) before executing new directive.

Sensor fusion latency and accuracy: If processed state estimates from perception modules are delayed or inaccurate, real-time feedback loop (DP2) may fail to detect cycle deviations. Mitigation: define Sensor Input Interface with latency and accuracy bounds; Vehicle validates sensor data and falls back to safe state if bounds are violated.

Powertrain/actuation parameter validation: If mission profile or environmental constraints provided at runtime exceed Vehicle's parameterized capabilities, Vehicle must reject the Tasking directive or degrade gracefully. Mitigation: implement runtime validation in Vehicle module; reject Tasking directives that violate actuation limits; export validation results via Telemetry interface.

State machine consistency under concurrent inputs: If Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Sensor Input interfaces update simultaneously, Vehicle state machine must have a deterministic arbitration order. Mitigation: define priority order (e.g., SafetyAlert > Tasking > Sensor Input) and implement atomic state transitions.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Sensor Input Interface: data types, update frequency, latency bounds, accuracy requirements, and fallback behavior if bounds are violated.
Formal specification of Actuation Command Interface: control signal types, command latency, actuation feedback, and error handling.
Formal specification of Constraint Input Interface (Tasking + SafetyAlert): constraint types, update semantics, priority order, and Vehicle response protocol.
State machine diagram with explicit handling of concurrent inputs (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Sensor Input) and state transition guards.

Authority model document: clear definition of Teleoperation authority (new directives only, no micro-management), Safety Services authority (constraint provision, not state veto), and Vehicle autonomy (state machine execution).
Runtime validation logic for mission profile and environmental constraints: acceptance criteria, rejection protocol, and Telemetry export of validation results.
Timing analysis: latency budget for each interface (Sensor Input, Actuation Command, Constraint Input) and cumulative impact on cycle time p95.
Safety analysis: FMEA or FTA for state machine transitions under SafetyAlert constraints; verification that Vehicle respects safety limits without introducing new failure modes.
